page: Microsoft Azure D2ns_v6 is a network-optimized virtual machine belonging to the Dnv6 server family. It is powered by an Intel Xeon 8573C processor utilizing the x86_64 architecture. The server features 2 dedicated vCPUs mapped to 1 physical core with Hyper-Threading enabled, providing 2 threads per core. The CPU operates at a clock speed of 4.2 GHz and includes a 260 MB L3 cache. It is equipped with 8.0 GB of system memory, yielding 4.0 GB of RAM per core. This instance does not include local storage or bundled GPUs, but it is Premium Storage capable.

Benchmark results indicate moderate single-core capabilities alongside constrained multi-core and memory performance. The stress-ng single-core CPU performance is rated as average at 1665.09, whereas the multi-core performance falls into the lower tier at 1706.89. Memory bandwidth is relatively limited, with read-only large block speeds at 17.21 GB/sec and write large block speeds at 12.81 GB/sec. Single-threaded compression tasks perform moderately, with gzip compression reaching 26.86 GB/sec. Database operations and Redis SET performance are on the lower end, with Redis achieving 2.44 million operations per second.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, the D2ns_v6 provides a dedicated Intel Xeon compute resource at a lower-tier resource density, making it a viable option for workloads requiring dedicated CPU allocation without massive memory or local storage overhead. Given its network-optimized design and 1:4 vCPU-to-RAM ratio, this server is suited for lightweight general-purpose workloads, basic web serving, single-threaded utility tasks, and small-scale network appliances that do not demand high memory bandwidth or local disk I/O.
description: Microsoft Azure D2ns_v6 is a network-optimized virtual machine from the Dnv6 family, featuring 2 dedicated vCPUs and 8.0 GB of RAM. Powered by an Intel Xeon 8573C processor running at 4.2 GHz, this x86_64 server provides a dedicated compute environment with a 260 MB L3 cache. It does not include bundled local storage or GPUs but supports Premium Storage. Benchmarks show average single-core performance, with stress-ng single-core scoring 1665.09, though multi-core and memory bandwidth metrics are relatively weak. The 1:4 vCPU-to-RAM ratio positions this instance as a cost-effective entry point for dedicated compute resources. It is best suited for lightweight general-purpose applications, basic web serving, and network-oriented workloads that do not require high memory throughput or local storage.
